paper_title: Don't Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills for QA and RAG

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds LLM responses in external evidence but treats the model as a passive consumer of search results: it never sees how the corpus is organized or what it has not yet retrieved, limiting its ability to backtrack or combine scattered evidence. We present Corpus2Skill, which distills a document corpus into a hierarchical skill directory offline and lets an LLM agent navigate it at serve time. The compilation pipeline iteratively clusters documents, generates LLM-written summaries at each level, and materializes the result as a tree of navigable skill files. At serve time, the agent receives a bird's-eye view of the corpus, drills into topic branches via progressively finer summaries, and retrieves full documents by ID. Because the hierarchy is explicitly visible, the agent can reason about where to look, backtrack from unproductive paths, and combine evidence across branches. On WixQA, an enterprise customer-support benchmark for RAG, Corpus2Skill outperforms dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG baselines across all quality metrics.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds LLM responses in external evidence but treats the model as passive consumer of search results: it never sees how the corpus is organized or what it has not yet retrieved, limiting its ability to backtrack or combine scattered evidence. We present CORPUS2SKILL, which distills document corpus into hierarchical skill directory offline and lets an LLM agent navigate it at serve time. The compilation pipeline iteratively clusters documents, generates LLM-written summaries at each level, and materializes the result as tree of navigable skill files. At serve time, the agent receives birds-eye view of the corpus, drills into topic branches via progressively finer summaries, and retrieves full documents by ID. Because the hierarchy is explicitly visible, the agent can reason about where to look, backtrack from unproductive paths, and combine evidence across branches. On WixQA, an enterprise customersupport benchmark for RAG, CORPUS2SKILL outperforms dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG baselines across all quality metrics. 1. Introduction Enterprise knowledge bases contain thousands of heterogeneous documents: support articles, product guides, policy documents, and FAQs (Gao et al., 2024). Large language models alone cannot answer questions about these corpora reliably: they lack access to private, frequently updated content and are prone to hallucinating plausible but incorrect details (Lewis et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2024). Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG; Lewis et al., 2020) was introduced to solve these problems by grounding LLM responses in retrieved evidence. By embedding the query, fetching similar passages from vector index, and condiPreprint. April 17, 2026. 1 Figure 1. Retrieve vs. Navigate. Traditional RAG passively feeds fixed passages to the LLM. CORPUS2SKILL distills the corpus into navigable skill hierarchy that the agent actively explores, backtracks, and drills into to locate evidence. tioning the models answer on the retrieved context, RAG enables LLMs to leverage enterprise-specific knowledge they were never trained on, providing factual, up-to-date answers while reducing hallucination. This pipeline has become the dominant paradigm for enterprise QA, customer support, and compliance workflows (Gao et al., 2024; Singh et al., 2025). Yet it presents structural limitation for more complex queries: the LLM never sees the forest for the trees. Consider support agent faced with the query How do switch my business from sole proprietorship to LLC on Wix? This query spans multiple topics: account types, payment provider configuration, and legal entity management, each covered in different sections of the knowledge base. flat retrieval system returns the top-k passages closest to the query embedding, which are likely to match on surface-level terms (sole proprietorship, LLC) but miss the operaDont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills tionally critical article explaining that account types cannot be changed directly and require contacting support. The LLM receives these fragments but has no understanding of how the corpus is organized, what topic areas exist beyond the retrieved set, or whether the best-matching documents were even in the retrieval window. It cannot reason about where else to look, whether its evidence is complete, or how the retrieved passages relate to the broader knowledge structure. In short, conventional RAG treats the LLM as passive consumer of search results, leaving the models planning and reasoning capabilities largely unused. Agentic RAG systems (Yao et al., 2023; Schick et al., 2023) partially address this by allowing the LLM to issue multiple search queries iteratively. But even with tool access, the agent operates without map: it must guess productive search terms, has no way to survey the corpus structure, and cannot systematically narrow its focus from broad topics to specific documents. Each search query is shot in the dark, guided only by the agents prior knowledge and whatever fragments previous queries returned. For the business-type query above, an agentic retrieval system would need to independently discover that the answer involves both the payments ecosystem and account verification policies topics whose connection is not apparent from keyword similarity alone. Hierarchical approaches such as RAPTOR (Sarthi et al., 2024) and GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024) organize documents into trees or graphs with LLM-generated summaries at internal nodes. These summaries capture thematic structure that flat embeddings miss. However, these systems still rely on embedding-based similarity search at query time: RAPTOR indexes all tree nodes in flat vector store, while GraphRAG requires graph database and entity extraction pipeline. The hierarchy enriches the retrieval candidates but remains invisible to the LLM at query time; the model still receives search results without understanding the organizational structure that produced them. More recent work pushes further: StructRAG (Li et al., 2025) restructures retrieved information into tables or graphs at inference time, and BookRAG (Wang et al., 2025) builds hierarchical indices with agent-based planners. Yet these systems still invoke embedding-based search at query time and do not give the agent direct access to the organizational structure itself. Meanwhile, parallel line of work on LLM agent skills (Wang et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2026) has shown that agents perform complex tasks more effectively when given structured, reusable knowledge modules that they can selectively load and follow. Agent platforms now support filesystembased skill packages1 that the agent discovers via metadata 1https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/ agents-and-tools/agent-skills and reads on demand, mechanism known as progressive disclosure (Section 2.3). This suggests an alternative to retrieval: rather than searching for passages, distill the corpus into navigable skill hierarchy and let the agent explore it. We propose exactly this: make the hierarchy itself the primary interface for the LLM. Rather than searching over hierarchy, the agent navigates through it. This is the core idea behind CORPUS2SKILL (Figure 1): one-time offline compilation phase transforms document corpus into navigable skill tree, and at serve time, the LLM agent browses this tree to find information, much like human navigating well-organized file system or table of contents. We evaluate CORPUS2SKILL on WixQA (Cohen et al., 2025), an enterprise customer-support benchmark designed for evaluating RAG systems over real-world knowledge bases. CORPUS2SKILL achieves the highest quality across all metrics, outperforming flat retrieval, RAPTOR, and multi-turn agentic RAG system, while requiring no embedding index or vector database at serve time. These results suggest complementary design point for knowledgegrounded systems: invest computation at compile time to make the corpus navigable, rather than solely at query time to make it searchable. We summarize our contributions as follows. 1. We identify corpus navigability as an underexplored dimension in current RAG systems: LLMs lack structured overview of the knowledge base, which limits their ability to systematically locate the best evidence. 2. We propose CORPUS2SKILL, compile-then-navigate framework: an offline pipeline transforms raw corpus into hierarchical skill directory via iterative clustering and LLM-based summarization, and at serve time an LLM agent navigates this hierarchy through file browsing and document lookup tools, requiring no embedding index or vector database at serve time. 3. We evaluate on WixQA (Cohen et al., 2025) against five baselines and conduct ablation studies on hierarchy structure, exploration budget, and serving model choice. 2. Background: Skills in LLM Agents 2.1. From Tools to Skills Modern LLM agents interact with external systems through tools: atomic, stateless functions such as web search or database queries (Schick et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2023). Tools give an agent the ability to act but do not tell it how to approach complex task. Agent skills fill this gap: reusable modules that package procedural knowledge or multi-step workflows into form the agent can load and follow (Jiang et al., 2026). Unlike tool, skill shapes reasoning over multiple steps, persisting across tool calls to guide the agents 2 Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills decisions throughout task. This distinction is increasingly important as agents tackle longer-horizon tasks where choosing the right sequence of tool calls requires domain-specific strategy, not just tool availability. Formally, Jiang et al. (2026) define skill as tuple = (C, π, T, R): an applicability condition C, an execution policy π, termination condition , and reusable interface R. This distinguishes skills from one-off plans (no reusable interface), episodic memories (no execution policy), and atomic tools (no multi-step structure). 2.2. Skill Representations Skills take several forms in practice: Code-as-skill. Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) stores successful action sequences as executable functions in growing library. Natural-language playbooks. SkillX (Wang et al., 2026) and EvoSkill (Alzubi et al., 2026) distill agent experience into structured textual instructions. Filesystem-based packages. directory containing structured instruction file (SKILL.md) alongside optional scripts and data assets. This is the pattern adopted by recent agent platforms and the one CORPUS2SKILL builds on. Code-based skills offer precision but require execution environments; natural-language skills are portable and interpretable at the cost of ambiguity. Filesystem-based packages balance the two by combining structured text with auxiliary resources that the agent can access on demand. 2.3. Progressive Disclosure Loading all skill content at startup would overwhelm the context window; loading nothing would leave the agent unable to choose. The filesystem pattern resolves this via progressive disclosure (Shneiderman, 1996): 1. Metadata (at startup): skill name and one-line description (<100 tokens each). 2. Instructions (on demand): the full SKILL.md is read when the agent selects skill. 3. Resources (as needed): supporting files remain on disk, consuming zero tokens until accessed. The agent thus maintains awareness of many skills while paying the token cost only for those actually used. 2.4. From Procedural Skills to Informational Skills In prior work, skills encode how to perform task: workflows, decision trees, or code patterns. CORPUS2SKILL repurposes the same mechanism to encode what corpus contains. Each skill directory represents topical partition, with SKILL.md and INDEX.md files that summarize its contents at increasing levels of detail. The agent follows the same progressive disclosure workflow (description SKILL.md INDEX.md document) but navigates an information hierarchy rather than executing procedural instructions. This reinterpretationfrom procedural to informational skillsis the central design insight of CORPUS2SKILL. 3. CORPUS2SKILL Framework CORPUS2SKILL operates in two phases: one-time offline compile phase that transforms raw document corpus into browsable skill tree, and per-query online serve phase where an LLM agent navigates this tree with full visibility into the corpus structure. Figure 2 illustrates the full architecture. 3.1. Problem Setup Let = {d1, . . . , dN } be corpus of documents. Given natural-language query q, the task is to produce an answer grounded in subset Dq of relevant documents. Standard RAG systems select Dq via retrieval function (q, D) Dq that operates as black box from the LLMs perspective: the model receives Dq but has no visibility into what contains or why Dq was chosen. CORPUS2SKILL replaces this opaque retrieval step with agent-driven navigation. The compile phase constructs hierarchical skill forest = {s1, . . . , sK} where each skill sk is navigable directory tree of summaries covering topical partition of D. Following the agentic skills formalism of Jiang et al. (2026), each skill can be viewed as tuple (C, π, T, R): the applicability condition is the skills summary (the agent reads it to decide relevance); the execution policy π is the navigation workflow (descend through summaries, then retrieve documents); the termination condition is the agents judgment that sufficient evidence has been gathered; and the reusable interface is the standardized file structure (SKILL.md, INDEX.md) and the get document tool. At serve time, the agent selects and navigates skills autonomously, making the document selection process transparent and steerable. 3.2. Compile Phase The compilation pipeline consists of four stages (implementation details and full prompts in Sections and C). Document loading and embedding. The compiler accepts directories containing standard document formats (.md, .txt, .json, .jsonl). Each document is assigned content-hash-based unique identifier, ensuring deterministic, collision-free IDs that are independent of the 3 Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills Figure 2. Corpus2Skill system architecture. The compile phase (top) embeds documents, builds multi-level cluster hierarchy with LLM summarization at each level, and materializes the result as forest of navigable skills. Root clusters map to skill directories (SKILL.md), sub-clusters map to group directories (INDEX.md), and leaf documents are stored in separate document store. The serve phase (bottom) uses an LLM agent with pre-compiled skills and two tools: file navigation (code execution) and document lookup (get document). source systems naming scheme. Documents are truncated to maximum character length and embedded using sentence embedding model (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019), producing dense vector for each document. These embeddings capture semantic similarity between documents and serve as the foundation for the subsequent clustering step. Iterative hierarchical clustering. We build multi-level hierarchy through an iterative bottom-up process controlled by two parameters: the branching ratio (target number of children per cluster) and the maximum number of top-level clusters K. At each level, the current set of embeddings is partitioned into n/p groups via K-Means (Lloyd, 1982) on L2-normalized vectors. Each resulting cluster is then summarized by an LLM that reads representative member texts and produces concise description of the clusters topical coverage, the types of questions the documents answer, and key terms or features mentioned. These summaries are re-embedded using the same sentence embedding model, and the resulting embeddings become the input to the next round of K-Means. This process repeats until the number of remaining clusters falls below K, yielding set of root clusters that form the top level of the hierarchy. This iterative embed-cluster-summarize loop is inspired by the recursive tree construction in RAPTOR (Sarthi et al., 2024), but adapted for our navigability goal. We use KMeans with hard assignments so that each document maps to exactly one path in the directory tree, and we materialize the hierarchy as browsable files rather than indexing it in flat vector store. These two choices, deterministic partitioning and filesystem materialization, are what enable agent-driven navigation at serve time. Small clusters below minimum size are merged into the nearest centroid to avoid orphan groups. Labeling. After the clustering hierarchy stabilizes, the LLM generates short (2 to 5 word) filesystem-safe label for each non-leaf node. These labels serve dual purpose: they provide human-readable directory names for inspection (e.g., wix-commerce-monetization) and, more importantly, they give the agent semantically meaningful entry points for topic routing. When the agent reads SKILL.md listing subgroups such as wix-payments-ecosystem or domain-dns-management, the labels alone often suffice to identify the relevant branch without reading full summaries. Skill tree construction. The hierarchy is materialized into filesystem structure designed for agent navigation: Root clusters become top-level skill directories, each containing SKILL.md with the cluster summary and listing of child groups. Sub-clusters become subdirectories with INDEX.md 4 Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills Full document files that list either further subgroups or leaf document identifiers alongside brief summaries of each document. stored externally in is documents.json file, referenced by document ID from within the navigation files. text This separation between navigation metadata and document content is deliberate architectural choice. Navigation files (SKILL.md, INDEX.md) remain small (typically under 2 KB each), containing only summaries and document identifiers. This keeps the agents per-step token cost low: reading an INDEX.md to survey 20 documents costs far fewer tokens than reading the documents themselves. Full document content is accessed only when the agent has narrowed its search, via dedicated get document tool call. The result is forest of independent skill trees, each representing topical domain within the corpus. The forest structure allows the agent to reason about topic relevance at the coarsest level before committing to branch. Skill file design. Each SKILL.md and INDEX.md follows uniform template with YAML frontmatter (name, description, level, num documents) that the Skills API reads for progressive disclosure. Compilation prompts produce routing-oriented summaries: the LLM identifies the topic area, question types, and key terms, so that each file functions as decision point for the navigating agent. Each file also embeds actionable next-step instructions (e.g., Use get document with the doc id), so the agent always knows which tool to invoke next. Figure 3 shows abridged examples of both file types. The SKILL.md (top) gives the agent single-glance overview of all subgroups within skill; the leaf INDEX.md (bottom) lists individual document IDs with concise titles for targeted retrieval. Unabridged versions at all three hierarchy levels are in Section B. Navigation complexity. The hierarchy depth grows as O(logp ): with branching ratio and documents, the agent traverses at most = logp levels to reach leaf. Note that this bound counts summaries inspected under an idealized single-path traversal; in practice, the actual token cost is higher because the Skills API includes previously loaded navigation files in each subsequent call, and the agent may explore multiple branches. The per-query costs reported in Section 4 reflect these real-world overheads. 100,000-document corpus with p=10 would require only one additional level (L=5). 3.3. Serve Phase At serve time, the pre-compiled skill directories are uploaded to Anthropics Skills API. The API implements progressive disclosure: skill names and one-line descriptions are pre-loaded into the agents context, but full file content is loaded only when the agent explicitly reads file. This design is critical for token efficiency. Pre-loading all navigation files would flood the context with thousands of tokens of irrelevant summaries; loading nothing would leave the agent blind to the corpus. Progressive disclosure strikes balance: the agent sees just enough (skill names and descriptions, approximately 200 tokens total) to make an informed first decision, then incrementally loads detail as it descends. Tools. The (1) receives code execution, which allows browsing SKILL.md tools: agent two Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills and INDEX.md files through view commands, providing the agent with full visibility into the hierarchy; and (2) get document(doc id), custom tool that retrieves the full text of document from the document store given its identifier. The two-tool design reflects the two types of information the agent needs: navigational (where to look) and evidential (what to cite). Keeping them separate means the agent can survey many document summaries cheaply before committing to the token cost of reading full documents. Navigation workflow. Because skill descriptions are preloaded, the agent begins each query with birds-eye view of the corpus, knowing what topics exist and roughly how many documents each topic covers. typical query proceeds in 2 to 3 turns: 1. The agent identifies the most relevant skill from preloaded descriptions and reads its SKILL.md to understand the subgroup structure. 2. The agent navigates into the relevant subgroups INDEX.md, which lists document identifiers with brief titles and summaries. 3. The agent calls get document to retrieve the full text of one or more promising documents and synthesizes grounded answer. At each step, the agent makes an informed decision guided If SKILL.md by summaries describing each branch. lists ten subgroups and the agent has only explored one, it knows nine remainan awareness that flat retrieval cannot provide. This structural visibility enables two behaviors: targeted backtracking (abandoning an unpromising branch for more relevant one) and cross-branch synthesis (combining evidence from multiple subgroups within or across skills). The system prompt enforces grounding rule (every claim must trace to retrieved document, not summary file) and concise-answer format (word limits, no preamble); see Section D. 4. Experiments 4.1. Setup Dataset. We evaluate on WixQA (Cohen et al., 2025), customer support QA benchmark built from the Wix knowledge base. The corpus contains 6,221 support articles covering website building, e-commerce, SEO, marketing, and platform features. The evaluation set consists of 200 expertwritten questions with gold-standard answers and gold article IDs. Compilation. We compile the WixQA corpus with branching ratio p=10 and maximum top-level clusters K=7. This produces 3-level hierarchy with 6 top-level skills, 665 navigation files, and 13 MB document store. Compilation takes 6.5 minutes on 32-CPU server. Baselines. All methods use the same LLM for answer generation and the same evaluation protocol. We compare five baselines spanning three retrieval paradigms: BM25 (sparse keyword retrieval (Robertson & Zaragoza, 2009)), Dense (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B (Zhang et al., 2025) with FAISS index (Johnson et al., 2019)), Hybrid (Reciprocal Rank Fusion (Cormack et al., 2009) of BM25 and Dense), RAPTOR (hierarchical tree retrieval (Sarthi et al., 2024) with UMAP+GMM+BIC clustering), and Agentic (an LLM agent with iterative access to BM25, Dense, and Hybrid tools). The first three are single-shot (top-5 passages, one LLM call); RAPTOR uses collapsed tree retrieval; Agentic is allowed up to 10 interaction rounds. Full implementation details are in Section H. Metrics. We report four lexical overlap metrics: Token F1 (harmonic mean of token-level precision and recall against the gold answer), BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) (n-gram precision with brevity penalty), ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2 (Lin, 2004) (unigram and bigram recall). We additionally report two LLM-judged metrics following the LLM-as-judge paradigm (Zheng et al., 2023), inspired by RAGAS (Es et al., 2024): Factuality measures how factually correct the generated answer is relative to the gold answer (15 scale, normalized to 01), and Context Recall measures how well the retrieved context covers the essential claims in the gold answer (same scale). We also report input token usage and per-query cost. Details on metric computation and fairness standardization across methods are in Section I. 4.2. Main Results Quality. Table 1 presents the main results. CORPUS2SKILL achieves the highest scores across all six quality metrics. On Token F1, CORPUS2SKILL scores 0.460, 19% relative improvement over Agentic (0.388) and 27% over Dense retrieval (0.363). On Factuality, CORPUS2SKILL (0.729) exceeds both Agentic (0.724) and RAPTOR (0.675). On Context Recall, CORPUS2SKILL leads by clear margin (0.652 vs. 0.616 for RAPTOR and 0.481 for Agentic), indicating that the navigation-based approach surfaces more relevant content than either vector search or multi-turn agentic retrieval. Hierarchical methods outperform flat retrieval. Both RAPTOR and CORPUS2SKILL substantially outperform the three flat retrieval baselines (BM25, Dense, Hybrid), confirming that hierarchical document organization improves answer quality. Ablation Studies We present two representative traces showing how CORPUS2SKILL navigates the hierarchy (Figures 4 and 5). Patterns. These traces illustrate two common navigation patterns: direct descent (Figure 4: skill subgroup leaf document) and cross-branch exploration (Figure 5: the agent visits multiple subgroups within the same skill to combine evidence). In both cases, the agents decisions are guided by summaries at each level. Full traces with raw tool calls are provided in Section E. We conduct three ablation studies to characterize how compile-time and serve-time design choices affect the quality-cost tradeoff. Cluster structure. We vary the branching ratio and topcluster count to produce hierarchies of different shapes  (Table 2)  . With p=5, the narrow branching ratio creates 4-level hierarchy with 3 top-level skills. With deeper hierarchies or larger corpora, the file count per skill can exceed the APIs 200-file limit. This reduces file count by up to 80% while preserving all navigational information. The narrow hierarchy slightly outperforms the default on all quality metrics (F1 0.461 vs. 0.460, Factuality 0.736 vs. 0.729, Context Recall 0.674 vs. 0.652) at modest 8% cost increase ($0.186 vs. $0.172 per query). The deeper hierarchy provides more fine-grained topic groupings, and the compact merged files give the agent denser view of the leaf-level documents in fewer navigation steps. In the wide-shallow variant (p=20), F1 drops 21% and Factuality drops 44% while costing 41% more. With only 2 extremely broad skills covering approximately 3,000 documents each, the SKILL.md summaries become too generic for effective topic routing. Agent exploration budget. We vary the maximum allowed interaction rounds while keeping the default skill tree  (Table 3)  . The exploration budget has minimal impact on quality: even budget of 5 achieves F1 of 0.453, only 1.5% below the full-budget configuration. Factuality is nearly identical across variants (0.721 to 0.748), and the marginal cost difference is negligible. The hierarchical structure is sufficiently well-organized that the agent rarely needs extended exploration. Serving LLM choice. We swap only the serving model while keeping the same compiled skill tree  (Table 4)  . smaller, cheaper model achieves comparable quality at roughly half the cost ($0.088 vs. $0.172 per query). While F1 drops by 8% and Factuality by 12%, Context Recall increases from 0.652 to 0.705, suggesting the cheaper model navigates the hierarchy more thoroughly, retrieving more relevant documents even if its answer synthesis is slightly less polished. At $0.088 per query, the cheaper model offers compelling cost-quality tradeoff. This also demonstrates that the compiled skill tree is robust to the choice of serving model: the quality of the hierarchy, not the sophistication of Cost. The per-query cost of CORPUS2SKILL is dominated by input tokens: the Skills API includes navigation file content in every call, so even relatively small SKILL.md and INDEX.md files accumulate significant token counts across turns. CORPUS2SKILL is best suited for high-value queries where answer quality justifies the cost premium, such as complex multi-hop support questions or compliance-critical lookups. For high-volume, low-stakes queries, single-shot retrieval or RAPTOR remain cost-effective. Prompt caching could substantially reduce this overhead by reusing repeated skill file content across queries within the same session. API constraints. The framework relies on Anthropics Skills API, which imposes limits of 8 skills per request, 200 files per skill, and 30 MB per skill. These constraints shape the hierarchy design: the 8-skill limit caps top-level clusters, and the 200-file budget constrains tree depth before requiring compact merging (Section 4.4). The WixQA corpus fits comfortably (6 skills, 133 files each), but larger corpora would require compact merging or multi-level partitioning. As Skills APIs evolve and constraints relax, even narrower hierarchies could improve navigation precision. Hard single-path clustering. Each document is assigned to exactly one cluster path, deliberate choice that enables filesystem materialization and deterministic navigation. However, documents that span multiple topics (e.g., billing and subscriptions) can only appear under one branch, creating routing bottleneck when queries target the other topic. Our failure analysis (Section G) confirms that toplevel routing errors account for the majority of failures. Soft or multi-parent assignment could mitigate this at the cost of duplicated content and more complex navigation; we leave this exploration to future work. Compilation. The one-time compilation costs $5$10 in LLM calls plus 6.5 minutes of wall-clock time, negligible for enterprise deployments handling thousands of daily queries. The pipeline is fully automated and deterministic given fixed random seeds, but does not yet support incremental updatesadding documents requires recompilation. 6. Related Work Cluster-based document organization. Scatter/Gather (Cutting et al., 1992) introduced the paradigm of navigating pre-computed cluster organization rather than matching by similarity, grounded in the cluster hypothesis (van Rijsbergen, 1979; Liu & Croft, 2004). Where Scatter/Gather required users to manually select and refine clusters in an 8 Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills interactive loop, CORPUS2SKILL delegates this navigation entirely to an LLM agent that reasons about cluster summaries at each level. Hierarchical summarization for retrieval. RAPTOR (Sarthi et al., 2024) recursively clusters and summarizes text chunks into tree, retrieving via layer-by-layer traversal or collapsed-tree vector search. CORPUS2SKILL shares the compile-time embed-cluster-summarize loop but materializes the hierarchy as navigable files and replaces fixed traversal with agentic navigation. GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024) builds knowledge graph with community-level summaries; unlike CORPUS2SKILL, it requires graph database at serve time. HiRAG (Huang et al., 2025) augments RAG with hierarchical knowledge graph that interleaves entity and community levels; it still relies on graph retrieval at query time rather than agent-driven file navigation. Retrieval-augmented generation. RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) grounds LLM responses in retrieved passages, with dense passage retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and lateinteraction models (Santhanam et al., 2022) pushing retrieval quality. Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2024) teaches the model to critique its own retrieval; IRCoT (Trivedi et al., 2023) interleaves retrieval with chain-of-thought reasoning for multi-hop questions, using intermediate reasoning steps to formulate better queries. Recent surveys (Gao et al., 2024; Singh et al., 2025) organize RAG along axes of granularity, timing, and agentic control. CORPUS2SKILL occupies distinct point on this spectrum: rather than improving retrieval, it replaces embedding-based search with agent-driven file navigation over pre-compiled hierarchy. Structure-aware and agentic RAG. StructRAG (Li et al., 2025) dynamically restructures retrieved information into tables, graphs, or catalogues at inference time, letting the LLM reason over structured views. BookRAG (Wang et al., 2025) builds hierarchical index mirroring documents table of contents and uses an agent-based planner to query it. A-RAG (Du et al., 2026) exposes hierarchical retrieval interfaces (keyword, semantic, chunk-read) to an LLM agent, and SPD-RAG (Akay et al., 2026) assigns dedicated subagent per document in multi-agent pipeline. NaviRAG (Dai et al., 2026) builds hierarchical view of documents and uses an LLM agent to actively navigate it, detecting information gaps and retrieving at the appropriate granularity. HCAG (Wu & Deng, 2026) constructs multi-resolution knowledge base offline and performs hierarchical retrieval with multi-agent discussion at query time. All of these systems retain some form of retrieval or search infrastructure at serve time. CORPUS2SKILL differs by materializing the hierarchy as static filesystem of skill files and navigating it with single agent using file browsing and document lookup rather than embedding-based search. Tool-use agents. Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023) and ReAct (Yao et al., 2023) established that LLMs can autonomously select and use external tools. CORPUS2SKILL extends this paradigm with domain-specific, read-only tools (file browsing and document lookup) over static hierarchy, making navigation deterministic and reproducible given the same compiled skill tree. Agent skill construction. Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) builds skill library from successful action sequences as code; SkillX (Wang et al., 2026) and EvoSkill (Alzubi et al., 2026) distill agent trajectories into reusable knowledge. These create skills from trajectories, whereas CORPUS2SKILL creates skills from document corpora via clustering and summarization, fundamentally different input modality. Positioning. CORPUS2SKILL combines offline hierarchical clustering, materialization as navigable skill files, and LLM-driven top-down navigation into system that replaces embedding-based retrieval with agent-driven file browsing at serve time. While NaviRAG and HCAG share the motivation of active hierarchical navigation, CORPUS2SKILL is distinguished by its filesystem-based skill representation, progressive disclosure via SKILL.md/INDEX.md files, and reliance on standard code-execution tool rather than custom retrieval APIs. Table 6 in the Appendix provides detailed feature comparison. 7. Conclusion CORPUS2SKILL demonstrates that giving an LLM agent structured, browsable overview of document corpus leads to higher-quality answers than providing it with retrieved passages alone. The key insight is that modern LLM agents are capable navigators when given the right map: pre-compiled hierarchy of summaries and topic clusters enables informed, top-down exploration that exploits the agents reasoning abilities rather than treating it as passive consumer of search results. Because the agent reads summaries describing each branch, it can make informed routing decisions and combine evidence across branches when queries span multiple topicsan advantage that flat retrieval, which offers no corpus overview, cannot match. While the approach incurs higher per-query cost due to navigation overhead, it achieves the highest quality on WixQA and suggests complementary design point for knowledgegrounded systems: invest computation at compile time to make the corpus navigable, rather than at query time to make it searchable. Hierarchy depth grows logarithmically (O(logp )), so the framework scales to larger corpora. Future work includes evaluation on more domains, incremental compilation, and prompt caching to cut query cost. 9 Dont Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: CORPUS2SKILL is designed for enterprise knowledgegrounded QA, setting where incorrect answers can lead to real-world harm (e.g., wrong compliance guidance or billing instructions). Like all LLM-based systems, it can produce confidently wrong answers when the agent navigates to irrelevant documents or misinterprets retrieved content. Hierarchical summaries produced during compilation may also expose corpus structure that organizations consider sensitive. Hard clustering means that documents spanning multiple topics are routed to single branch, creating blind spots that may disproportionately affect certain query types. Finally, because compilation is batch process, the system cannot reflect real-time corpus updates, risking stale or outdated answers until recompilation occurs. We recommend human-in-the-loop verification for high-stakes deployments.

Documents using 622M-parameter Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, model producing 1024-dimensional embeddings. We use the document prompt template with batch size 32. This model is chosen for its strong performance-to-size ratio and ability to run on CPU. Summarization. The compilation pipeline uses direct LLM API calls (not an agent loop) for summarization and labeling. Level-1 summaries condense up to 15 document texts (600 characters each); deeper-level summaries condense up to 20 child summaries (300 characters each). Summarization is performed concurrently using async calls with semaphore of 20 for batch efficiency. File counts. The compilation produces 665 navigation files and documents.json file storing the full text of all 6,221 documents (13 MB). Total output size is 16 MB. Read the SKILL.md of the 1-2 most relevant skills for your query. 2. Drill into the most relevant sub-group: Read its INDEX.md. 3. At the leaf level, INDEX.md lists document IDs with brief titles. Pick the most relevant document IDs. 4. Call get_document with each relevant doc_id to retrieve the full text. 5. Read at least one full document before answering. ## Tools - Code execution: Use ls and cat to navigate the skills hierarchy. - get_document(doc_id): Retrieve the full text of document by its ID. The doc_id values are listed in leaf-level INDEX.md files. ## Answer Format - First sentence = direct answer. The serving agent receives the following system prompt: No preamble. # Corpus-Grounded Support Agent # (Hierarchical Navigation) You are knowledge agent that answers questions by navigating hierarchical skill directory. You explore structured file tree where documents are organized into topic clusters. ## Hard Rules - Every factual claim must trace to document you retrieved via get_document. - SKILL.md files are NAVIGATION AIDS -- they tell you where to look, not what to say. - Never fabricate steps, URLs, prices, or specifics not found in documents. - Never guess. If you cannot find relevant content after thorough exploration, say so. ## Navigation Strategy Your skills directory has this structure: skill-XX-topic/ SKILL.md <- top-level summary + index of contents - Factual questions: 1-3 sentences (80 words max). - Procedural questions: numbered steps only (150 words max). - Plain text. No bold, headers, or dividers. - One approach only. Do not present alternatives. - Never add "contact support" or closing remarks. E. Full Navigation Traces We present complete navigation traces with raw tool calls for four queries from the evaluation set. Trace 1: Direct descent (Factuality 1.0, CtxR 1.0). The agent correctly identified the general topic area but over-retrieved from neighboring leaf groups. Navigation mechanism. Scatter/Gather relies on human users to iteratively select clusters; RAPTOR and GraphRAG delegate navigation to fixed algorithms (vector similarity or graph traversal). CORPUS2SKILL is unique in delegating navigation to an LLM agent that can reason about which branch to explore, backtrack when path is unproductive, and combine evidence across branches. This agent-driven navigation is what enables the trial-and-error behavior illustrated in Figures 4 and 5. Serve-time infrastructure. All prior systems require dedicated infrastructure at query time: cluster index, vector database, or graph database. CORPUS2SKILL eliminates this dependency entirelythe only runtime component is the LLM itself, which receives pre-compiled skill files via the API. This LLM only property simplifies deployment but shifts the cost burden to input tokens, as discussed in Section 5. G. Failure Analysis We examine the 62 queries (31%) on which CORPUS2SKILL scores Factuality0.4 or Context Recall0.2. By comparing each querys retrieved documents against the gold article IDs (using prefix matching, since CORPUS2SKILL uses truncated content hashes), we identify three failure modes: Navigation miss (38 queries). The agent retrieves documents but none match the gold article, indicating it descended into the wrong branch of the hierarchy. For example, query about reordering CMS collection items is routed to general site-editing skill instead of the CMSspecific one; query about blocking time slots in staff schedule navigates to generic booking group rather than the staff-management leaf. These misroutes occur at the top level of the hierarchy, where broad skill summaries fail to distinguish closely related topics. Partial navigation (19 queries). The agents retrieved set includes the gold document, yet scores remain low. In these cases the gold article is retrieved alongside several non-relevant documents, diluting the context provided to 18 Synthesis error (3 queries). The agent retrieves contextually relevant documents (Context Recall0.6) but produces factually incorrect answer, typically by over-generalizing across multiple articles or misinterpreting conditional instructions as universal. No documents retrieved (2 queries). Two queries (How do clear my browser cache? and Gmail password reset question) resulted in zero retrieved documents. Both have gold articles in the corpus (Wix publishes help articles for these topics), but the agent could not locate matching skill branch and correctly declined to answer rather than hallucinating. Takeaway. The dominant bottleneck is initial topic routing: 38 of 62 failures stem from the agent choosing the wrong top-level branch. The compact-mode ablation (Section 4.4), which produces narrower top-level clusters, partially mitigates this by providing finer-grained topic separation at the first navigation step. H. Context Recall standardization. critical design decision is the standardization of Context Recall measurement across different retrieval paradigms (single-shot, multi-turn, and navigation-based). For fair comparison, all methods follow the same protocol: (1) collect the last 5 documents or passages retrieved by the method; (2) join them with separator tokens; (3) truncate to uniform 8,000-character window. The LLM judge receives this standardized context. This ensures that single-shot baselines contribute their top-5 retrieved passages, Agentic contributes the last 5 documents from its multi-turn retrieval, and CORPUS2SKILL contributes the last 5 documents retrieved via get document tool calls. Cost metrics. Input token count is the total number of tokens sent to the LLM across all turns. Per-query cost is computed using the models published per-token pricing (input and output tokens combined). For single-shot methods, cost reflects one LLM call; for multi-turn methods (Agentic, CORPUS2SKILL), it reflects all turns including tool-call reasoning. Hybrid. We combine BM25 and Dense scores using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (Cormack et al., 2009) with k=60 and equal weighting (α=0.5). The fused ranking selects the top-5 passages, which are passed to single LLM call. RAPTOR. We faithfully implement the RAPTOR algorithm (Sarthi et al., 2024): UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018) for dimensionality reduction, Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) soft clustering (McLachlan & Peel, 2000) with BIC-based cluster count selection (Schwarz, 1978), and recursive summarization of clusters. We use the same embedding model (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) as the Dense baseline for consistency. The system prompt instructs the agent to search first, then answer based on accumulated evidence, choosing BM25 for exact terms and product names, dense for conceptual queries, and hybrid as default strategy. The agent can issue up to 10 rounds of iterative retrieval, reformulating sub-queries based on intermediate results. Evidence is accumulated across all turns; when the agent stops, it produces structured JSON response containing the answer and curated top-5 references selected from the full evidence pool. For evaluation fairness, context recall is computed over the last 5 retrieved documents (matching the context window of single-shot baselines). This baseline tests whether agentic multi-turn retrieval over traditional search infrastructure can match navigation-based exploration. I. Evaluation Metrics Lexical metrics. Token F1 tokenizes both the predicted and gold answers into word tokens and computes the harmonic mean of token-level precision and recall. BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) measures modified n-gram precision (up to 4-grams) with brevity penalty to penalize overly short outputs. ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2 (Lin, 2004) measure unigram and bigram recall respectively, capturing how much of the gold answers content appears in the prediction. LLM-judged metrics. Both Factuality and Context Recall use an LLM judge (Zheng et al., 2023) following the RAGAS framework (Es et al., 2024), scoring on 15 scale normalized to 01. Factuality evaluates whether the claims in the generated answer are factually consistent with the gold answer, independent of the retrieved context. Context Recall evaluates whether the retrieved context contains sufficient information to answer the question, by checking how many essential claims in the gold answer can be attributed
